column1: Comprehensive Historical Timeline of Turan and Danavas

column2: Key Developments
Early Indo-European migrations begin.
Possible origins of Danu concept as a primordial water goddess in proto-Indo-European mythology.
Development of early Sintashta and Andronovo cultures in Central Asia, potential ancestors of later Indo-Iranian peoples.
Formation of early Vedic culture in the Indian subcontinent.
Early Indo-European migrations. Possible origins of Danu concept as a primordial water goddess13.
Composition of the earliest hymns of the Rig Veda begins.
Early conceptualization of devas and asuras with fluid boundaries between groups.
First mentions of Danu in Rig Veda 1.32.9, associated with primordial waters.

Composition of early Rig Veda verses. Earliest references to Danu in Rig Veda 1.32.9. Vritra mentioned as son of Danu. In books 2-7 of Rig Veda, Vrtra is not considered an asura or demon. No mention of Danu or Danavas in books 2-7 of Rig Veda. Early flexible conceptualization of deities and asuras with fluid boundaries between groups.
Composition of early Rig Veda verses continues.
Vritra mentioned as son of Danu in Rig Veda.
Books 2-7 of Rig Veda composed, showing no mention of Danu or Danavas.
Development of early Iranian cultures in Central Asia and Iranian plateau.
Composition of later Rig Veda verses; more antagonistic portrayal of Vritra and other Danavas emerges.

Development of Vedic mythology including genealogy of Asuras. Brahma's six sons established in mythology. Marichi fathers Kashyapa. Kashyapa marries thirteen of Daksha's daughters, including Diti and Danu. Diti's children become Daityas; Danu's children become Danavas. Etymology of Danavas from Vedic word "Da" (to give), connoting generosity according to Ananda Coomaraswamy.
Brahma's six sons established in mythology; Marichi fathers Kashyapa.
Kashyapa marries thirteen daughters of Daksha, including Diti and Danu.
Diti's children become Daityas; Danu's children become Danavas.
Etymology of Danavas from the Vedic word "Da" (to give), connoting generosity according to Ananda Coomaraswamy.

Evolution of Danu concept as primordial waters in which her son Vritra hides to deceive Indra. Vritra described both as "son of Danāyu" in Mahābhārata and "son of Danu" in Ṛigveda, showing textual variations. Gradual shift from ambiguous moral status to more antagonistic portrayal of Danavas. One hundred Danavas mentioned in texts.
Vritra described both as "son of Danāyu" in Mahābhārata and "son of Danu" in Ṛigveda, showing textual variations.
Gradual shift from ambiguous moral status to more antagonistic portrayal of Danavas.
One hundred Danavas mentioned in texts.
Development of Zoroastrian texts; Tuiryas (Turanians) mentioned alongside Airyas (Aryans).
Zoroaster preaches to Turanians; some accept his message while others reject it.
Development of Fravashis concept with righteous men and women among both Aryas and Tuiryas.

Earliest mention of Turan in Farvardin Yasht. Tuiryas (Turanians) mentioned alongside Airyas (Aryans), Sairimas (Sarmatians), Sainus (Sacae) and Dahis (Dahae). Zoroaster preaches to Turanians; some accept his message while others reject it. Development of Fravashis concept with righteous men and women among both Aryas and Tuiryas.
Earliest mention of Turan in Farvardin Yasht; hostility between Tuirya and Airya indicated in Farvardin Yasht where Fravashis support battle against Danus (clan of Tura people).

Mythological story of Danavas' exile from Svarga during Satya Yuga. Danavas take refuge in the Vindhya range. Compilation of Hindu epics including Mahabharata begins. Genealogical history of Asuras formalized. Danavas portrayed as half-brothers to the devas and daityas. Hostility between Tuirya and Airya indicated in Farvardtn Yast where Fravashis of the Just provide support in battle against the Danus (clan of Tura people).
Compilation of Hindu epics including Mahabharata begins; genealogical history of asuras formalized.
Danavas portrayed as half-brothers to devas and daityas; growing complexity in their portrayal begins.
Composition of early Puranic literature elaborates on Danava genealogy and myths.

Son of Emperor Fereydun (Tūr/Turaj) established as founder of Turanians in Iranian mythology. Fraternal conflict between Tūr and Īraj creates Iran-Turan rivalry. Turan comprises Kopet Dag region, Atrek valley, parts of Bactria, Sogdia, and Margiana. Growing complexity in portrayal of Danavas in Hindu texts, with some depicted as virtuous and others as malevolent.
Growing complexity in portrayal of Danavas in Hindu texts, with some depicted as virtuous and others malevolent.
Development of Buddhist Asura myths, influenced by earlier Vedic concepts4.

Growing distinction between Danavas and devas in later Puranic literature, though boundaries remain complex. Some Danavas described as devotees of Shiva or Vishnu. Individual Danavas given more nuanced characterizations. Development of stories where some Danavas receive boons from major deities. Ambiguous moral status maintained for certain Danava figures.
Composition of Natyashastra. Danavas depicted as evil demons interfering with sacred performances4.

Sassanian Empire defines Turan in opposition to Iran. Turan designated as land of enemies to the northeast. Memory of Turanians kept alive through nomadic invasions. Danavas firmly established as opposing Hindu deities in Puranic literature, though exceptions exist. Development of stories where Danavas challenge the cosmic order but sometimes serve important cosmic functions.

Turks pushed westward by other tribes. First contact between Iranians and Turks. Continuing development of Danava mythology with stories of confrontations with major Hindu gods like Indra, Vishnu, and Shiva.
Expansion of Gupta Empire. Possible influence on portrayal of Danavas in Hindu mythology3.

Identification of Turanians with Turks develops. Some Danavas portrayed as receiving redemption or elevation through devotion to particular deities, showing evolution from purely antagonistic portrayal.

Persians identify lands northeast of Khorasan beyond Oxus as Turan. "Turk" and "Turanian" used interchangeably in Islamic Era texts. Al-Masudi clarifies Afrasiyab was not a Turk. Further development of regional variations in Danava stories across different parts of India.
Development of Zoroastrian texts. Possible influence on later conceptions of Danavas as adversaries5.
Development of Tantric traditions. Some Danavas incorporated into esoteric practices3.

Myth of Afrasiyab adopted by the Qarakhanid dynasty. Turkic self-identification with Turanian heritage begins. Increasing incorporation of Danavas into sectarian Hindu mythologies with some Danavas becoming important devotees in Shaiva and Vaishnava traditions.
Turkic self-identification with Turanian heritage begins under Qarakhanid dynasty.

Complex relationship between Danavas and Hindu deities fully develops in later Hindu texts. Distinction becomes more nuanced than simple opposition. Some Danavas venerated in local traditions. Danava ancestry claimed by certain regional dynasties or communities in South Asia.
Composition of later Puranas. Further elaboration of Danava myths and characters3.

Term "Turan" used to refer to the domain of the Uzbek empire. Geopolitical concept in conflict with Safavids. Continued evolution of Danava mythology in regional Hindu traditions with some Danavas portrayed as moral exemplars or teachers.
Bhakti movement influences portrayal of some Danavas as devotees7.
"Histoire Générale des Huns, des Turcs, des Mongols" by Joseph de Guignes published. Early European conception of Turkish/Turanian peoples.

Max Müller identifies "Turanian language family." Groups non-Indo-European, non-Semitic languages. Includes Altaic, Dravidian, Uralic, Japanese, Korean languages. Academic study of Hindu mythology begins to systematize understanding of Danavas.
Colonial-era scholarship begins systematic study of Danava myths2.
J.W. Jackson describes "Turanid race." Depicts Turanians as "impersonation of material power."
German "Map of Iran and Turan" produced. Shows Turan encompassing modern Central Asia.
Ármin Vámbéry's "Sketches of Central Asia" published. Proposes common origins of Turkic groups as one race.
"L'histoire de l'Asie" by Leon Cahun published. Stresses role of Turks in "carrying civilization to Europe."

Term borrowed into Western languages as designation for Central Asia. "Turan Plain" becomes geographical term. Hungarian Turanism develops. Western scholarship begins comparative studies of Indo-Iranian and Indo-Aryan mythologies, noting parallels between Danu/Danavas and Turan concepts.

Turkish nationalists adopt "Turanian" for pan-Turkic ideology. Important aspect of Turkish Nationalist Movement Party ideology. Term largely obsolete in academic linguistics. Survives in names (Turan, Turaj) and cultural references. Modern Hindu traditions continue to present complex portraits of Danavas, with some regional temples and festivals dedicated to specific Danava figures once seen as adversaries but now venerated. Academic studies highlight the shifting nature of deity-asura relationships throughout Hindu mythological history.
Modern Hindu traditions reinterpret certain Danava figures, presenting them positively.
Comparative mythology studies link Danu/Danava concepts to other Indo-European traditions5.
Modern reinterpretations of Danava myths in popular culture and contemporary Hinduism7.

column1: Here's a revised timeline table with numeric date labels, removing circa marks and including known sources as citations:
The earliest written records of Turkish languages date back to the 8th century CE, found in the Orkhon Inscriptions9.
The Proto-Turkic language, ancestor of all Turkic languages, is estimated to have originated in Asia around 500 BCE1.
Old Turkic, the first attested form of Turkic, has its earliest known records dating to the 7th century CE1.
The name "Türk" was first used as a political identity during the Göktürk Khaganate period in the 6th century CE7.
Tarsus is mentioned in Xenophon's Anabasis (around 400 BCE) as a great and wealthy city8.
The linguistic history of Turkic languages can be followed in written sources from the 8th century CE onward10.

This timeline demonstrates the evolution of Turkic-related terms and place names across different regions and time periods, reflecting the complex linguistic and cultural interactions throughout Eurasia.
